You
You are a developer migrating from Git who wants to experiment with a feature branch without modifying the original commits. The repository has a `feature` branch with a commit that adds a new function to `app.py`. Your task is to: 1. Duplicate the commit pointed to by the `feature` bookmark. 2. Rebase the duplicated commit so that it is a child of the `main` bookmark. 3. Create a new bookmark called `feature-experiment` pointing to this newly duplicated and rebased commit. 4. Leave the original `feature` bookmark and its commit unchanged. To verify your work, the `feature-experiment` bookmark must point to a commit that has the same exact changes as `feature`, but its parent must be the commit pointed to by `main`.

I'm leaning towards `jj duplicate` for the initial duplication step, so now I'm reading the help info to verify parameters.\n\n\n","state":"done"},{"type":"tool-executeCommand","toolCallId":"0bEjga723B6gXw99","state":"output-available","input":{"command":"jj duplicate --help","cwd":"home/user/repo"},"output":{"output":"Create new changes with the same content as existing ones\r\n\r\nWhen none of the `--destination`, `--insert-after`, or `--insert-before` arguments are provided,\r\ncommits will be duplicated onto their existing parents or onto other newly duplicated commits.\r\n\r\nWhen any of the `--destination`, `--insert-after`, or `--insert-before` arguments are provided, the\r\nroots of the specified commits will be duplicated onto the destination indicated by the arguments.\r\nOther specified commits will be duplicated onto these newly duplicated commits. If the\r\n`--insert-after` or `--insert-before` arguments are provided, the new children indicated by the\r\narguments will be rebased onto the heads of the specified commits.\r\n\r\nUsage: jj duplicate [OPTIONS] [REVSETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [REVSETS]...\r\n          The revision(s) to duplicate (default: @)\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -d, --destination <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to duplicate onto (can be repeated to create a merge commit)\r\n\r\n  -A, --insert-after <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to insert after (can be repeated to create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: after]\r\n\r\n  -B, --insert-before <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to insert before (can be repeated to create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: before]\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML\r\n          expression.
